front.title: AIR/1: Offline-Verifiable Agent Intent Receipts over x402

abstract.t: An Agent Intent Receipt (AIR) is a self-certifying record that an HTTP request was fulfilled by a named provider and paid for on-chain over the x402 payment protocol. A receipt can be verified offline, by any party, without trusting the party that issued it and without access to the issuer's systems. This document defines the AIR/1 object model, its canonical JSON serialization, its extensible signature-suite framework, the verification procedure, and the associated IANA registry. Two initial signature suites are defined, one for secp256k1/EVM signers and one for Ed25519/Solana signers.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The x402 protocol lets a client pay for a single HTTP request on-chain with no account and no API key: the server answers an unpaid request with HTTP 402 (Payment Required), the client returns a payment authorization, and the server serves the result once settlement is arranged. After settlement the client holds a result, but nothing that independently proves what was requested, who fulfilled it, and that payment occurred.

This document defines the Agent Intent Receipt, version 1 (AIR/1): a compact, signed JSON object that binds those three facts together so a third party (an auditor, a marketplace, or a dispute resolver) can check them after the fact, offline, with no access to the issuer.
AIR/1 is transport- and platform-neutral. It does not depend on any particular gateway, facilitator, or chain beyond the abstractions defined here.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: The party that authorizes and funds the on-chain payment for a request.
The party that submits the payment on-chain and produces a settlement transaction. In x402 this is typically a third-party settlement service.
The provider or gateway that fulfills the request and signs the receipt. The issuer's cryptographic identity is carried in the signature block ().
Any party that checks a receipt. A verifier needs only public information.
An AIR/1 object as defined in .

section.name: Trust Model
section.t: AIR/1 is trust-minimizing: three independent parties back three independent facts, and no single party can forge the whole receipt.

table.name: Facts, backers, and how a verifier checks each

tr.td: Look up settlement.tx_hash on the named chain
This receipt was issued by X

section.t: The facilitator holds its own settlement key and lands the payment on-chain; the receipt never needs that key. The issuer signs the whole receipt with its own key, identified by signature.signer. The facilitator and the issuer are distinct roles and are generally distinct entities.

The payment authorization signed by the payer (for example, an EIP-3009 transferWithAuthorization over EIP-712 ) is a separate signature, by a separate key, over a separate payload, and is out of scope for this document. AIR/1 attests to fulfillment, not to the payment authorization. Implementations MUST NOT reuse the payer's payment-authorization signature as the receipt signature.

section.name: Receipt Object
section.t: An unsigned receipt is a JSON object with the following members.

tr.td: Identifier of the provider that fulfilled the intent.
Lowercase SHA-256 hex digest of the canonical intent ().
Lowercase SHA-256 hex digest of the canonical result ().
Human-readable name of the issuing service. The cryptographic identity is signature.signer.

section.t: Implementations MAY include additional top-level members. A verifier MUST preserve unknown members verbatim; unknown members are covered by the signature ().

section.name: The settlement Object
section.t: settlement records what happened on-chain. Its members are informational for verification; the authoritative source is the chain itself.

tr.td: Settlement transaction hash. To be confirmed on-chain.
Chain identifier, e.g. eip155:8453 (Base) or solana:mainnet.
Amount settled, in the asset's smallest unit, as a decimal string.
Identifier of the facilitator that settled the payment.

section.name: Canonical Serialization and Hashing
section.t: Hashes and signatures are computed over a canonical byte encoding so that independent implementations agree. The canonical form of a value is produced by the following rules, applied recursively. These rules are compatible with JCS for the value subset used by this specification.

ol.li: Object member names are sorted in ascending order by Unicode code point.
No insignificant whitespace is emitted. The value separator is U+002C (,) and the name/value separator is U+003A (:).
Output is UTF-8. Characters outside the ASCII range are emitted literally and MUST NOT be \u-escaped, except where JSON string escaping is required by .

A number whose value is an integer (for example 500.0) is emitted without a fractional part or exponent (500). Other numbers are emitted in their shortest round-tripping decimal form. See the number-precision caveat in .
The literals true, false, and null are emitted as-is.

section.t: The intent_hash is the lowercase hex SHA-256 digest of the canonical encoding of the intent object. The result_hash is computed the same way over the result object.

section.name: Signature Block
section.t: A signed receipt carries a signature object with these members.

tr.td: Signature-suite identifier from the AIR Signature Suites registry ().
The issuer's public identity, encoded as the suite defines.
The signature, encoded as the suite defines.

section.name: Signable Bytes
section.t: The signable bytes are the canonical encoding () of the receipt object with the signature member removed and all other members, known and unknown, included. Signing the canonical whole object, rather than a subset or a delimiter-joined string, is mandatory ().

section.name: secp256k1-eip191
section.t: The signable bytes () are signed as an EIP-191 personal message: the signer prepends the string "\x19Ethereum Signed Message:\n" || len(message) and signs the Keccak-256 digest of that concatenation using secp256k1 ECDSA . The leading 0x19 byte provides domain separation so the signed bytes cannot also be a valid Ethereum transaction. signer is the Ethereum address. A verifier recovers the address from the signature and compares it to signer case-insensitively.
This suite reuses the key and signing primitive an EVM wallet already has, so no new key material is introduced for receipts.

section.name: ed25519
section.t: The signable bytes are signed directly with an Ed25519 secret key, with no additional envelope. signer is the base58 encoding of the 32-byte public key; value is the base64 of the 64-byte detached signature. This is the native signature scheme on Solana.

section.name: Verification
section.t: Given a receipt, a verifier:

ol.li: Selects the suite for signature.algorithm. An unrecognized algorithm, or a missing value or signer, makes the signature invalid.
Reconstructs the signable bytes () and verifies signature.value against signature.signer using the selected suite.
If the original intent is available, recomputes intent_hash and compares.
If the original result is available, recomputes result_hash and compares.
Optionally pins the issuer by requiring signature.signer to equal an expected value (case-insensitively for EVM addresses).

section.t: A receipt is valid when the signature verifies, the version is "AIR/1", the signer matches any configured pin, and no supplied hash mismatches. Hash checks that the caller does not supply are skipped, not failed.

Confirming the payment itself is out of scope for receipt verification. A verifier that requires proof of payment MUST look up settlement.tx_hash on the named chain and confirm the amount, asset, and recipient. AIR/1 attests that the issuer asserts a given settlement; the chain is authoritative on whether it happened.

section.name: Example
section.t: A signed EVM receipt (whitespace added for readability; the signed bytes use the canonical form of ):

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests that IANA create a new registry as described below.

section.name: AIR Signature Suites Registry
section.t: IANA is requested to create the "AIR Signature Suites" registry. The registry governs the values of the algorithm member of the AIR/1 signature object ().
The registration policy is Specification Required . Each entry contains:

dl.dd: The string value used in signature.algorithm. Values MUST match the ABNF 1*( %x21-7E ) (printable US-ASCII, no spaces) and are compared as exact, case-sensitive byte strings.
How signature.signer is encoded for this suite.
How signature.value is encoded for this suite.
A stable reference to the defining specification.

section.t: The initial contents of the registry are:

tr.td: secp256k1 ECDSA with EIP-191 personal-message envelope

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Sign the whole object. An implementation MUST NOT sign a subset of fields or a delimiter-joined string; doing so invites field-injection and cross-field ambiguity. signs the canonical whole, which is the only supported mode.

The receipt is not proof of payment. A valid signature proves only that the issuer asserts a settlement. For value-bearing decisions a verifier MUST confirm settlement.tx_hash on-chain, as described in .
Pin the issuer. An unpinned valid signature says only that some holder of some key signed the receipt. A verifier that knows who should have signed MUST pin signature.signer to the expected identity.

Canonicalization is load-bearing. Two encoders that disagree on number normalization, member ordering, or string escaping will disagree on hashes and signatures. Implementations MUST follow exactly. The number normalization in assumes intent and result payloads carry no numbers that require more precision than an integer-valued float can represent; producers SHOULD encode high-precision or large-magnitude quantities as strings.

Key separation. The issuer's receipt-signing key SHOULD be distinct in role from any payment key. Even where the same curve is used (for example secp256k1 on EVM), the payment authorization and the receipt use different envelopes and different payloads and MUST NOT be conflated ().

Replay and freshness. AIR/1 does not itself prevent a receipt from being presented more than once. issued_at and the on-chain uniqueness of settlement.tx_hash give a verifier the means to detect duplicates; applications that require single-use semantics MUST enforce them out of band.
Algorithm agility and downgrade. Because the suite is named in the receipt, a verifier MUST fail closed on unrecognized suites and MUST NOT silently accept a weaker suite than a policy requires.

author.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

front.title: EIP-712: Typed structured data hashing and signing
SEC 2: Recommended Elliptic Curve Domain Parameters

section.name: Test Vectors
section.t: This appendix provides deterministic golden test vectors. Any conforming implementation MUST reproduce the exact hash and canonical byte values below from the given inputs, and MUST produce byte-identical signatures given the listed test keys. The keys here are well-known test keys and MUST NOT be used on a live chain.

section.name: Inputs
section.t: Intent object (hashed to intent_hash):
Result object (hashed to result_hash):
Settlement object embedded in the receipt:
Fixed metadata: issued_at = "2026-07-16T00:00:00Z", issuer = "issuer.example".
Test keys (test-only, never used on a live chain):

section.name: secp256k1-eip191 (Base) Vector
section.t: Signer address: 0x70997970C51812dc3A010C7d01b50e0d17dc79C8
Canonical signable bytes (UTF-8), the exact string that is hashed and signed:
Signature (base64): EwCqgPNvVSjPtby8BMtxLLo+i0q7cc4TT4ScANVn1lBOTw263W1ntIcYNhq19k4MrLWgWHzY4pfr6ap/lcfwuhw=
Signature (hex): 1300aa80f36f5528cfb5bcbc04cb712cba3e8b4abb71ce134f849c00d567d6504e4f0dbadd6d67b48718361ab5f64e0cacb5a0587cd8e297ebe9aa7f95c7f0ba1c

section.name: ed25519 (Solana) Vector
section.t: Signer public key (base58): FAe4sisG95oZ42w7buUn5qEE4TAnfTTFPiguZUHmhiF
Canonical signable bytes (UTF-8), the exact string that is signed:
Signature (base64): teFQgXN6pI5Vud5E8YpvDUhej9aX4PIaAFd+oQp6Cagkq3W5OiOPPE/Yt+lod6fo6d/epeure2jCGeNJhlU9CA==
Signature (hex): b5e15081737aa48e55b9de44f18a6f0d485e8fd697e0f21a00577ea10a7a09a824ab75b93a238f3c4fd8b7e96877a7e8e9dfdea5ebab7b68c219e34986553d08

section.name: Reference Implementation
section.t: The agent_intent_x402.receipt module of the agent-intent-x402 project implements this specification: canonicalize, hash_object, build_receipt, sign_receipt, verify_receipt, and the secp256k1-eip191 and ed25519 signer/verifier suites. It is provided for interoperability testing and is not normative.

section.name: Acknowledgments
section.t: This work distills the offline-verifiable-receipt idea explored by earlier agent-intent projects into a platform-neutral, x402-native form.
